question_html: A 12.5 eV electron beam is used to bombard gaseous hydrogen at room temperature. What series of wavelengths will be emitted?
question_text: A 12.5 eV electron beam is used to bombard gaseous hydrogen at room temperature. What series of wavelengths will be emitted?
explanation: Given energy of electron beam E = {tex}12.5 \mathrm{eV}=12.5 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex} . Also, the energy of the gaseous hydrogen in its ground state at room temperature is −13.6 eV. When gaseous hydrogen is bombarded with an electron beam, the energy of the gaseous hydrogen becomes = −13.6 + 12.5 eV i.e., −1.1 eV. Thus, the electrons of hydrogen will be raised to the third orbit, because this energy is approximately equal to the energy of gaseous hydrogen(-1.5eV). Now, the following transitions are possible n = 3 to n = 2 then n = 2 to n = 1 or n = 3 to n = 1 Therefore, three spectral lines will be obtained. These are {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\lambda=6.56 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} or 656 nm. This line lies in the Balmer series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\lambda=1.22 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} or 122 nm. This line lies in the Lyman series. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} or {tex}\lambda=1.03 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} or 103 nm. This line lies in the Lyman series.

question_html: Suppose you are given a chance to repeat the alpha particle scattering experiment using a thin sheet of solid hydrogen in place of the gold foil. (Hydrogen is a solid at temperatures below 14 K). What results do you expect?
question_text: Suppose you are given a chance to repeat the alpha particle scattering experiment using a thin sheet of solid hydrogen in place of the gold foil. (Hydrogen is a solid at temperatures below 14 K). What results do you expect?
explanation: The nucleus of a hydrogen atom is a proton (mass {tex}1.67 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg{/tex} ) which has only about one-fourth of the mass of an alpha particle {tex}\left( {6.64 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}kg} \right){/tex} . Because the alpha particle is more massive, it won't bounce back in even a head on collision with a proton. It is like a bowling ball colliding with a ping pong ball at rest. Thus, there would be no large angle scattering in this case. In Rutherford's experiment, by contrast, there was large angle scattering because a gold nucleus is more massive than an alpha particle. The analogy there is a ping pong ball hitting a bowling ball at rest.

question_html: According to the classical electromagnetic theory, calculate the initial frequency of the light emitted by the electron revolving around a proton in hydrogen atom.
question_text: According to the classical electromagnetic theory, calculate the initial frequency of the light emitted by the electron revolving around a proton in hydrogen atom.
explanation: We know that the velocity of an electron moving around a proton in a hydrogen atom in an orbit of radius is 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –11 m is 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s. Thus, the frequency of the electron moving around the proton is given by ; {tex}v=\frac{v}{2 \pi r}=\frac{2.2 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}{2 \pi\left(5.3 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\approx {/tex} 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz According to the classical electromagnetic theory, we know that the frequency of the electromagnetic waves emitted by the revolving electrons is equal to the frequency of its revolution around the nucleus. Thus the initial frequency of the light emitted is given by 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz.

question_text: In Rutherford’s nuclear model of the atom, the nucleus (radius about 10 –15 m) is analogous to the sun about which the electron move in orbit (radius {tex}\approx {/tex} 10 –10 m) like the earth orbits around the sun. If the dimensions of the solar system had the same proportions as those of the atom, would the earth be closer to or farther away from the sun than actually, it is? The radius of the earth’s orbit is about 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 11 m. The radius of the sun is taken as 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m.
explanation: The ratio of the radius of electron’s orbit to the radius of a nucleus is (10 –10 m)/(10 –15 m) = 10 5 , that is, the radius of the electron’s orbit is 10 5 times larger than the radius of the nucleus. If the radius of the earth’s orbit around the sun were 10 5 times larger than the radius of the sun, the radius of the earth’s orbit would be 10 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 13 m. This is more than 100 times greater than the actual orbital radius of the earth. Thus, the earth would be much farther away from the sun. It implies that an atom contains a much greater fraction of empty space than our solar system does.

question_text: Write the mathematical forms of three postulates of Bohr's theory of the hydrogen atom. Using them prove that, for an electron revolving in the {tex}{n}^{\text {th }}{/tex} orbit, the radius of the orbit is proportional to {tex}{n}^{2}{/tex} , and the total energy of the atom is proportional to {tex}\left(\frac{1}{{n}^{2}}\right){/tex} .
explanation: Mathematical form of postulates of Bohr's Theory {tex} \text { } E_n=\frac{-13.6}{n^2} {eV} {/tex} {tex}{L}={mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}{h} v={E}_{{f}}-{E}_{{i}}{/tex} (a) {tex}\frac{{mv}^2}{{r}}=\frac{{Ze}^2}{{r}^2}{/tex} ...(1) {tex} {mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi} {/tex} ...(2) Solving (1) & (2) {tex} {r}={n}^2\left(\frac{{~h}}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{{me}^2} {/tex} ...(3) Since energy in the orbit {tex}E_n=\frac{-e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex} Using eq (3) {tex}E_n=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 \varepsilon_0{ }^2 h^2}{/tex} or {tex}{E}_{{n}} \propto \frac{1}{{n}^2}{/tex}

solution_html:
Mathematical form of postulates of Bohr's Theory

  1. {tex} \text { } E_n=\frac{-13.6}{n^2} {eV} {/tex}
  2. {tex}{L}={mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex}
  3. {tex}{h} v={E}_{{f}}-{E}_{{i}}{/tex}

(a) {tex}\frac{{mv}^2}{{r}}=\frac{{Ze}^2}{{r}^2}{/tex} ...(1)
{tex} {mvr}=\frac{{nh}}{2 \pi} {/tex} ...(2)
Solving (1) & (2)
{tex} {r}={n}^2\left(\frac{{~h}}{2 \pi}\right)^2 \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{{me}^2} {/tex} ...(3)
Since energy in the orbit {tex}E_n=\frac{-e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}{/tex}
Using eq (3) {tex}E_n=\frac{-m e^4}{8 n^2 \varepsilon_0{ }^2 h^2}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{n_B^2}\right){/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_3}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{n_C^2}\right){/tex} ...(iii)
From (i)
{tex}\frac{R}{n_B^2}=\frac{1}{\lambda_1}+\frac{R}{n_C^2}{/tex}
Substituting in (ii)
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{R}{n_A^2}-\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{R}{n_C^2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}+\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{A^2}}-\frac{1}{n_{C^2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}+\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\frac{1}{\lambda_3}{/tex}

  2. Calculate the orbital period in each of these levels.
question_text: Using the Bohr's model, calculate the speed of the electron in a hydrogen atom in the n = 1, 2 and 3 levels. Calculate the orbital period in each of these levels.
explanation: Now, {tex}v = \frac{c}{n}\alpha {/tex} , where {tex}\alpha = \frac{{2\pi K{e^2}}}{{ch}}{/tex} = 0.0073 {tex}{v_1} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{1} \times 0.0073 = 2.19 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex} {tex}{v_2} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 1.095 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex} {tex}{v_3} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 7.3 \times {10^5}m/s{/tex} Orbital period, {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi r}}{\upsilon }{/tex} As {tex}{r_1} = 0.53 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = \frac{{2\pi \times 0.53 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{2.19 \times {{10}^6}}} = 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s{/tex} As r 2 = 4 r 1 and {tex}{\upsilon _2} = \frac{1}{2}{\upsilon _1}{/tex} {tex}{T_2} = 8\;{T_1} = 8 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 1.216 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex} As r 3 = 9r 1 and {tex}{\upsilon _3} = \frac{1}{3}{\upsilon _1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {T_3} = 27{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = 27 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 4.1 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex}
solution: Now, {tex}v = \frac{c}{n}\alpha {/tex} , where {tex}\alpha = \frac{{2\pi K{e^2}}}{{ch}}{/tex} = 0.0073 {tex}{v_1} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{1} \times 0.0073 = 2.19 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex} {tex}{v_2} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 1.095 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex} {tex}{v_3} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 7.3 \times {10^5}m/s{/tex} Orbital period, {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi r}}{\upsilon }{/tex} As {tex}{r_1} = 0.53 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = \frac{{2\pi \times 0.53 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{2.19 \times {{10}^6}}} = 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s{/tex} As r 2 = 4 r 1 and {tex}{\upsilon _2} = \frac{1}{2}{\upsilon _1}{/tex} {tex}{T_2} = 8\;{T_1} = 8 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 1.216 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex} As r 3 = 9r 1 and {tex}{\upsilon _3} = \frac{1}{3}{\upsilon _1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {T_3} = 27{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = 27 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 4.1 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Now, {tex}v = \frac{c}{n}\alpha {/tex},
    where {tex}\alpha = \frac{{2\pi K{e^2}}}{{ch}}{/tex} = 0.0073
    {tex}{v_1} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{1} \times 0.0073 = 2.19 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex}
    {tex}{v_2} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 1.095 \times {10^6}m/s{/tex}
    {tex}{v_3} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{3} \times 0.0073 = 7.3 \times {10^5}m/s{/tex}
  2. Orbital period, {tex}T = \frac{{2\pi r}}{\upsilon }{/tex}
    As {tex}{r_1} = 0.53 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex}
    {tex}{T_1} = \frac{{2\pi \times 0.53 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{2.19 \times {{10}^6}}} = 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s{/tex}
    As r2 = 4 r1 and {tex}{\upsilon _2} = \frac{1}{2}{\upsilon _1}{/tex}
    {tex}{T_2} = 8\;{T_1} = 8 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 1.216 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex}
    As r3 = 9r1 and {tex}{\upsilon _3} = \frac{1}{3}{\upsilon _1}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {T_3} = 27{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = 27 \times 1.52 \times {10^{ - 16}}s = 4.1 \times {10^{ - 15}}s{/tex}

question_html:
A narrow beam of protons, each having 4.1 MeV energy is approaching a sheet of lead (Z = 82). Calculate:

  1. the speed of a proton in the beam, and
  2. the distance of its closest approach
question_text: A narrow beam of protons, each having 4.1 MeV energy is approaching a sheet of lead (Z = 82). Calculate: the speed of a proton in the beam, and the distance of its closest approach
explanation: KE of proton = 4.1 Mev = 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 J Mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -21 kg {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2{/tex} {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K E}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex} = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 J For the distance of closest approach KE of proton = PE of system 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 = {tex}\frac{k e \times 82 e}{\gamma}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 82 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex} = 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 = 2.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 m
solution: KE of proton = 4.1 Mev = 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 J Mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -21 kg {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2{/tex} {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K E}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex} = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 J For the distance of closest approach KE of proton = PE of system 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 = {tex}\frac{k e \times 82 e}{\gamma}{/tex} {tex}r=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 82 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex} = 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 = 2.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 m
solution_html:
1. KE of proton = 4.1 Mev = 4.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
    = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 J
    Mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-21 kg
    {tex}K E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2{/tex}
    {tex}v=\sqrt{\frac{2 K E}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex}
    = 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 J
  2. For the distance of closest approach
    KE of proton = PE of system
    6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 = {tex}\frac{k e \times 82 e}{\gamma}{/tex}
    {tex}r=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 82 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2}{6.56 \times 10^{-13}}{/tex}
    = 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16
    = 2.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 m

Take radius of first orbit of hydrogen atom as {tex}0.5 \overset oA{/tex}.
question_text: In Bohr model of hydrogen atom, an electron is revolving in second orbit. Find the value of: angular momentum of electron, radius of the orbit, and kinetic energy of electron. Take radius of first orbit of hydrogen atom as {tex}0.5 \overset oA{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} for {tex}n=2{/tex} {tex}L=\frac{2 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 3.14}{/tex} {tex}=2.11 \times 10^{-34} {kg} \ {m}^{2} {s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}r_{n}=n^{2} r_{0}{/tex} {tex}r_{2}=4(0.5 \overset oA){/tex} {tex}r_{2}=2 \overset 0A{/tex} Total energy of electron {tex}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex} {tex}E=-3.4 {eV} (n=2){/tex} {tex}K=-E{/tex} {tex}K=3.4 {eV}{/tex}
solution: {tex}L=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} for {tex}n=2{/tex} {tex}L=\frac{2 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 3.14}{/tex} {tex}=2.11 \times 10^{-34} {kg} \ {m}^{2} {s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}r_{n}=n^{2} r_{0}{/tex} {tex}r_{2}=4(0.5 \overset oA){/tex} {tex}r_{2}=2 \overset 0A{/tex} Total energy of electron {tex}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} {eV}{/tex} {tex}E=-3.4 {eV} (n=2){/tex} {tex}K=-E{/tex} {tex}K=3.4 {eV}{/tex}

    {tex}=2.11 \times 10^{-34} {kg} \ {m}^{2} {s}^{-1}{/tex}

explanation: {tex}E=-\frac{13 \cdot 6}{n^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2{ }^2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right)=R{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {max }}}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)=\frac{3 R}{4} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\max }}{\lambda_{\min }}=\frac{4}{3 R} \times R=\frac4 3{/tex}

{tex}E=-\frac{13 \cdot 6}{n^2}{/tex}

question_html: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron revolving in the first excited state of hydrogen atom. The ground state energy of the hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV.
question_text: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron revolving in the first excited state of hydrogen atom. The ground state energy of the hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV.
explanation: Energy of the electron in the first excited state E 1 = {tex}-\frac{13.6}{2^2} e V{/tex} = -3.4 eV = -3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = -5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Associated kinetic energy = Negative of total energy K = 5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h} {p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 5.44 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1} {2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(99.008)^{\frac {1}{2}} \times 10^{-25}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.663 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 0.663 nm = 6.63 A o Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron is 6.63 A o
solution: Energy of the electron in the first excited state E 1 = {tex}-\frac{13.6}{2^2} e V{/tex} = -3.4 eV = -3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = -5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Associated kinetic energy = Negative of total energy K = 5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h} {p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 5.44 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1} {2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(99.008)^{\frac {1}{2}} \times 10^{-25}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.663 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 0.663 nm = 6.63 A o Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron is 6.63 A o
solution_html:
Energy of the electron in the first excited state
E1 = {tex}-\frac{13.6}{2^2} e V{/tex} = -3.4 eV
= -3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
= -5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Associated kinetic energy = Negative of total energy
K = 5.44 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h} {p}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 5.44 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1} {2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(99.008)^{\frac {1}{2}} \times 10^{-25}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
{tex}\approx{/tex} 0.663 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m = 0.663 nm = 6.63 Ao
Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron is 6.63 Ao

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2nd excited state of hydrogen atom. The ground state energy of the hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV.
question_text: Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2 nd excited state of hydrogen atom. The ground state energy of the hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV.
explanation: Energy of the electron in the second excited state = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{3^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -1.51 eV Kinetic energy (K) in this state = 1.51 eV de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.51 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63}{(43.97)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 10 -9 m = 1nm Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2 nd excited state of hydrogen is 1nm.
solution: Energy of the electron in the second excited state = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{3^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = -1.51 eV Kinetic energy (K) in this state = 1.51 eV de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.51 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63}{(43.97)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} = 10 -9 m = 1nm Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2 nd excited state of hydrogen is 1nm.
solution_html:
Energy of the electron in the second excited state = {tex}\frac{-13.6}{3^2} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
= -1.51 eV
Kinetic energy (K) in this state = 1.51 eV
de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{\left(2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.51 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.63}{(43.97)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
= 10-9 m = 1nm
Hence, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the 2nd excited state of hydrogen is 1nm.

subject: Physics
question_html:
An electron in a hydrogen atom makes transitions from orbits of higher energies to orbits of lower energies.

  1. When will such transitions result in (a) Lyman (b) Balmer series?
  2. Find the ratio of the longest wavelength in Lyman series to the shortest wavelength in Balmer series.
question_text: An electron in a hydrogen atom makes transitions from orbits of higher energies to orbits of lower energies. When will such transitions result in (a) Lyman (b) Balmer series? Find the ratio of the longest wavelength in Lyman series to the shortest wavelength in Balmer series.
explanation: Transition result in Lyman series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 1 orbit Transition result in Balmer series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 2 orbit Longest wavelength in Lyman Series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R \cdot\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=\frac{3 R}{4}{/tex} Shorted wavelength in Balmer Series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_S}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} Ratio {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_S}=\frac{4 }{3 R} \times \frac{R}{4}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution: Transition result in Lyman series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 1 orbit Transition result in Balmer series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 2 orbit Longest wavelength in Lyman Series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R \cdot\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=\frac{3 R}{4}{/tex} Shorted wavelength in Balmer Series {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_S}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{4}{/tex} Ratio {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_S}=\frac{4 }{3 R} \times \frac{R}{4}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Transition result in Lyman series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 1 orbit
    Transition result in Balmer series if electron will jump from a higher energy orbit to n = 2 orbit
  2. Longest wavelength in Lyman Series
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R \cdot\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=\frac{3 R}{4}{/tex}
    Shorted wavelength in Balmer Series
    {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_S}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_1{ }^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{R}{4}{/tex}
    Ratio {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_S}=\frac{4 }{3 R} \times \frac{R}{4}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

question_html:
An electron is in the third excited state in a hydrogen atom. It undergoes transitions to the lower energy states.

question_html: State Bohr's quantization condition of angular momentum. Calculate the shortest wavelength of the Bracket series and state to which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does it belong.
question_text: State Bohr's quantization condition of angular momentum. Calculate the shortest wavelength of the Bracket series and state to which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does it belong.
explanation: Bohr’s quantization condition of angular momentum: According to Bohr, the electron can revolve in certain discrete orbits called stationary orbits. The total angular momentum (L) of the revolving electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} . Thus, angular momentum, L = mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex} where n = 1, 2, 3.... Brackett series is obtained when an electron jumps to fourth orbit (n 1 = 4) from any outer orbit (n 2 = 5, 6, 7.....) of the hydrogen atom. The wavelength of a spectral line is given as {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} = RZ {tex}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex} where R is Rydberg’s constant (1.097 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -1 ) and Z is the atomic number (Z = 1 for hydrogen atom). For Brackett series, n 1 = 4 and n 2 = 5, 6, 7… Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex} For the shortest wavelength, n 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} . Therefore, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-0\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}= \frac{R}{16}=\frac{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{16}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{16}{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{/tex} = 14.585 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1458.5 nm. The shortest wavelength of the Brackett series is 1458.5 nm. This wavelength belongs to infrared range of electromagnetic spectrum.

Bohr’s quantization condition of angular momentum: According to Bohr, the electron can revolve in certain discrete orbits called stationary orbits. The total angular momentum (L) of the revolving electron is an integral multiple of {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}.
Thus, angular momentum, L = mvr = {tex}\frac{nh}{2 \pi}{/tex}
where n = 1, 2, 3....
Brackett series is obtained when an electron jumps to fourth orbit (n1 = 4) from any outer orbit (n2 = 5, 6, 7.....) of the hydrogen atom.
The wavelength of a spectral line is given as
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} = RZ{tex}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex}

Therefore,
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right){/tex}
For the shortest wavelength, n2 = {tex}\infty{/tex}. Therefore,
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]=R\left(\frac{1}{4^{2}}-0\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}= \frac{R}{16}=\frac{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{16}{1.097 \times 10^{7}}{/tex} = 14.585 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1458.5 nm.
The shortest wavelength of the Brackett series is 1458.5 nm.
This wavelength belongs to infrared range of electromagnetic spectrum.

question_html:
The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6eV

  1. What is the potential energy of an electron in the 3rd excited state?
  2. If the electron jumps to the ground state from the 3rd excited state, calculate the wavelength of the photon emitted.
question_text: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6eV What is the potential energy of an electron in the 3 rd excited state? If the electron jumps to the ground state from the 3 rd excited state, calculate the wavelength of the photon emitted.
explanation: The energy of an electron in nth orbit is given by {tex}{E_n} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}eV{/tex} For 3 rd excite state, n = 4 {tex}\therefore {E_4} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{16}} = - 0.85eV{/tex} Required energy to jump electron to the ground state from the 3 rd excited state E = E 4 - E 1 {tex} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} - \left( { - \frac{{13.6}}{{{1^2}}}} \right){/tex} = - 0.85 + 13.6 = 12.75 eV {tex}\therefore {/tex} Wavelength of the photon emitted as {tex}\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{E}\left( {As,\;E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{19.878 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{20.4}} = 0.974 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} {tex} = 974\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}
solution: The energy of an electron in nth orbit is given by {tex}{E_n} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}eV{/tex} For 3 rd excite state, n = 4 {tex}\therefore {E_4} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{16}} = - 0.85eV{/tex} Required energy to jump electron to the ground state from the 3 rd excited state E = E 4 - E 1 {tex} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} - \left( { - \frac{{13.6}}{{{1^2}}}} \right){/tex} = - 0.85 + 13.6 = 12.75 eV {tex}\therefore {/tex} Wavelength of the photon emitted as {tex}\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{E}\left( {As,\;E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{19.878 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{20.4}} = 0.974 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} {tex} = 974\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}
solution_html:
The energy of an electron in nth orbit is given by
{tex}{E_n} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{n^2}}}eV{/tex}

  1. For 3 rd excite state, n = 4
    {tex}\therefore {E_4} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{16}} = - 0.85eV{/tex}
  2. Required energy to jump electron to the ground state from the 3 rd excited state
    E = E4 - E1
    {tex} = - \frac{{13.6}}{{{4^2}}} - \left( { - \frac{{13.6}}{{{1^2}}}} \right){/tex}
    = - 0.85 + 13.6 = 12.75 eV
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Wavelength of the photon emitted as
    {tex}\lambda = \frac{{hc}}{E}\left( {As,\;E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }} \right){/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{6.63 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{12.75 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{19.878 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{20.4}} = 0.974 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 974\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}

question_text: The radius of the innermost electron orbit of a hydrogen atom is 5.3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -11 m. What are the radii of the n = 2 and n = 3 orbits?

Then, let r2 be the radius of an orbit at n = 2.
Then, r2 = (n)2r1
As shown in the diagram:

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–11 m
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 2.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–10 m

question_html: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, what is the distance of closest approach to the nucleus of a 7.7 MeV {tex}\alpha - {/tex}particle before it comes momentarily to rest and reverses its direction?
question_text: In a Geiger-Marsden experiment, what is the distance of closest approach to the nucleus of a 7.7 MeV {tex}\alpha - {/tex} particle before it comes momentarily to rest and reverses its direction?
explanation: The key idea here is that throughout the scattering process, the total mechanical energy of the system consisting of an α-particle and a gold nucleus is conserved. The system’s initial mechanical energy is E i , before the particle and nucleus interact, and it is equal to its mechanical energy E f when the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle momentarily stops. The initial energy E i is just the kinetic energy K of the incoming {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. The final energy E f is just the electric potential energy U of the system. The potential energy U can be calculated from Eq. Let d be the centre-to-centre distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and the gold nucleus when the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is at its stopping point. Then we can write the conservation of energy E i = E f as {tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{d}=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex} Thus the distance of closest approach d is given by:- {tex}d=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} K}{/tex} The maximum kinetic energy found in α-particles of natural origin is 7.7 MeV or 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –12 J. Since {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 N m 2 /C 2 . Therefore with e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 C, Z = 79 we have, {tex}d=\frac{(2)\left(9.0 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2} \mathrm{79}}{1.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{J}}{/tex} d (Au) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m = 30 fm. (1 fm (i.e. fermi) = 10 –15 m.) The radius of gold nucleus is, therefore, less than 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m. This is not in very good agreement with the observed result as the actual radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm. The cause of discrepancy is that the distance of closest approach is considerably larger than the sum of the radii of the gold nucleus and the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. Thus, the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle reverses its motion without ever actually touching the gold nucleus.

The potential energy U can be calculated from Eq. Let d be the centre-to-centre distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and the gold nucleus when the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is at its stopping point. Then we can write the conservation of energy E i = E f as {tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{d}=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex} Thus the distance of closest approach d is given by:- {tex}d=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} K}{/tex} The maximum kinetic energy found in α-particles of natural origin is 7.7 MeV or 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –12 J. Since {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 N m 2 /C 2 . Therefore with e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 C, Z = 79 we have, {tex}d=\frac{(2)\left(9.0 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2} \mathrm{79}}{1.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{J}}{/tex} d (Au) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m = 30 fm. (1 fm (i.e. fermi) = 10 –15 m.) The radius of gold nucleus is, therefore, less than 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –14 m. This is not in very good agreement with the observed result as the actual radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm. The cause of discrepancy is that the distance of closest approach is considerably larger than the sum of the radii of the gold nucleus and the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. Thus, the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle reverses its motion without ever actually touching the gold nucleus.
solution_html:
The key idea here is that throughout the scattering process, the total mechanical energy of the system consisting of an α-particle and a gold nucleus is conserved. The system’s initial mechanical energy is Ei, before the particle and nucleus interact, and it is equal to its mechanical energy Ef when the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle momentarily stops. The initial energy Ei is just the kinetic energy K of the incoming {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle. The final energy Ef is just the electric potential energy U of the system. The potential energy U can be calculated from Eq. Let d be the centre-to-centre distance between the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle and the gold nucleus when the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle is at its stopping point. Then we can write the conservation of energy Ei = Ef as
{tex}K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{d}=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex}
Thus the distance of closest approach d is given by:-
{tex}d=\frac{2 Z e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} K}{/tex}
The maximum kinetic energy found in α-particles of natural origin is 7.7 MeV or 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–12 J. Since {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = 9.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 N m2/C2. Therefore with e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 C, Z = 79 we have,
{tex}d=\frac{(2)\left(9.0 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2} \mathrm{79}}{1.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{J}}{/tex}
d (Au) = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–14 m = 30 fm. (1 fm (i.e. fermi) = 10–15 m.)
The radius of gold nucleus is, therefore, less than 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–14 m. This is not in very good agreement with the observed result as the actual radius of gold nucleus is 6 fm. The cause of discrepancy is that the
distance of closest approach is considerably larger than the sum of the radii of the gold nucleus and the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle. Thus, the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle reverses its motion without ever actually touching the gold nucleus.

question_html: The ground state energy of an atom is - 13.6 eV. The photon emitted during the transition of an electron from n - 3 to n = 1 state, is incident on a photosensitive material of unknown work function. The photoelectrons are emitted from the materials with a maximum kinetic energy of 9 eV. Calculate the threshold wavelength of the material used.
question_text: The ground state energy of an atom is - 13.6 eV. The photon emitted during the transition of an electron from n - 3 to n = 1 state, is incident on a photosensitive material of unknown work function. The photoelectrons are emitted from the materials with a maximum kinetic energy of 9 eV. Calculate the threshold wavelength of the material used.
explanation: For a transition from n = 3 to n = 1 state, the energy of the emitted photon, hv = E 2 - E 1 {tex}=13.6\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right] \mathrm{eV}=12.1 \mathrm{eV}{/tex} From Einstein's photoelectric equation, hv = K max + W 0 {tex}\therefore {/tex} W 0 = hv - K max = 12.1 - 9 = 3.1 eV Threshold wavelength, {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{h c}{W_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{3.1 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
solution: For a transition from n = 3 to n = 1 state, the energy of the emitted photon, hv = E 2 - E 1 {tex}=13.6\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right] \mathrm{eV}=12.1 \mathrm{eV}{/tex} From Einstein's photoelectric equation, hv = K max + W 0 {tex}\therefore {/tex} W 0 = hv - K max = 12.1 - 9 = 3.1 eV Threshold wavelength, {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{h c}{W_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{3.1 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
For a transition from n = 3 to n = 1 state, the energy of the emitted photon,
hv = E2 - E1
{tex}=13.6\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right] \mathrm{eV}=12.1 \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
From Einstein's photoelectric equation,
hv = Kmax + W0
{tex}\therefore {/tex} W0 = hv - Kmax = 12.1 - 9 = 3.1 eV
Threshold wavelength, {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{h c}{W_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{3.1 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}

question_html: It is found experimentally that 13.6 eV energy is required to separate a hydrogen atom into a proton and an electron. Compute the orbital radius and the velocity of the electron in a hydrogen atom.
question_text: It is found experimentally that 13.6 eV energy is required to separate a hydrogen atom into a proton and an electron. Compute the orbital radius and the velocity of the electron in a hydrogen atom.
explanation: Total energy of the electron in hydrogen atom is –13.6 eV = –13.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 J = –2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –18 J. Thus from Eq., we have {tex}-\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} = E This gives the orbital radius {tex}r=-\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} E}=-\frac{\left(9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{N} \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(-2.2 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}\right)}{/tex} = 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –11 m The velocity of the revolving electron can be computed from Eq. with m = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –31 kg, {tex}{1\over 2 }mv^2 ={e^2\over 4\pi\epsilon_0 r^2}{/tex} thus velocity of electron is given by :- {tex}v=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m r^2}}=2.2 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution: Total energy of the electron in hydrogen atom is –13.6 eV = –13.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 J = –2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –18 J. Total energy of the electron in hydrogen atom is –13.6 eV = –13.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 J = –2.2 {tex}\times{/tex}10–18 J.
Thus from Eq., we have
{tex}-\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex}= E
This gives the orbital radius
{tex}r=-\frac{e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} E}=-\frac{\left(9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{N} \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right)\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)^{2}}{(2)\left(-2.2 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}\right)}{/tex}
= 5.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–11 m

question_html:
The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about –3.4 eV.

  1. What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state?
  2. What is the potential energy of the electron in this state?
  3. Which of the answers above would change if the choice of the zero of potential energy is changed?
question_text: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about –3.4 eV. What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state? What is the potential energy of the electron in this state? Which of the answers above would change if the choice of the zero of potential energy is changed?
explanation: Given: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about –3.4 eV. The kinetic energy of the electron in this state = negative of the total energy = -E Kinetic energy of the electron in this state = -(-3.4)eV = + 3.4 eV Potential energy is given as the negative of the twice of the kinetic energy U = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} (3.4) eV U = -6.8eV Hence the potential energy of the electron in the given state is − 6.8 eV. If the choice of the zero of potential energy is changed, then the value of potential energy of the system also changes and as we know the total energy is the sum of kinetic energy as well as potential energy. Therefore, the potential energy will also change.
solution: Given: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about –3.4 eV. The kinetic energy of the electron in this state = negative of the total energy = -E Kinetic energy of the electron in this state = -(-3.4)eV = + 3.4 eV Potential energy is given as the negative of the twice of the kinetic energy U = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} (3.4) eV U = -6.8eV Hence the potential energy of the electron in the given state is − 6.8 eV. If the choice of the zero of potential energy is changed, then the value of potential energy of the system also changes and as we know the total energy is the sum of kinetic energy as well as potential energy. Therefore, the potential energy will also change.
solution_html:
1. Given: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about –3.4 eV.
    The kinetic energy of the electron in this state = negative of the total energy = -E
    Kinetic energy of the electron in this state = -(-3.4)eV = + 3.4 eV
  2. Potential energy is given as the negative of the twice of the kinetic energy U = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} (3.4) eV
    U = -6.8eV
    Hence the potential energy of the electron in the given state is − 6.8 eV.
  3. If the choice of the zero of potential energy is changed, then the value of potential energy of the system also changes and as we know the total energy is the sum of kinetic energy as well as potential energy. Therefore, the potential energy will also change.

question_html: Using Rutherford model of the atom, derive the expression for the total energy of the electron in hydrogen atom. What is the significance of total negative energy possessed by the electron?
question_text: Using Rutherford model of the atom, derive the expression for the total energy of the electron in hydrogen atom. What is the significance of total negative energy possessed by the electron?
explanation: The Rutherford nuclear model of the atom describes the atom as an electrically neutral sphere consisting of a very small, massive and positively charged nucleus at the centre surrounded by the revolving electrons in their respective dynamically stable orbits. The electrostatic force of attraction F, between the revolving electrons and the nucleus provides the requisite centripetal force (F c ) to keep them in their orbits. Thus, for a dynamically stable orbit in a hydrogen atom F c = F e {tex}\frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ Z = 1] Thus, the relation between the orbit radius and the electron velocity is {tex}r = \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} The kinetic energy (K) and electrostatic potential energy (U) of the electron in hydrogen atom are {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 8 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} and {tex}U = - \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} (The negative sign in U signifies that the electrostatic force is attractive in nature.) Thus, the total mechanical energy E of the electron in a hydrogen atom is {tex}E = K + U = \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 8 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r } - \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r } = - \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 8 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} The total energy of the electron is negative. This implies the fact that the electron is bound to the nucleus. If E were positive, an electron will not follow a dosed orbit around the nucleus and it would leave the atom.
solution: The Rutherford nuclear model of the atom describes the atom as an electrically neutral sphere consisting of a very small, massive and positively charged nucleus at the centre surrounded by the revolving electrons in their respective dynamically stable orbits. The electrostatic force of attraction F, between the revolving electrons and the nucleus provides the requisite centripetal force (F c ) to keep them in their orbits. Thus, for a dynamically stable orbit in a hydrogen atom F c = F e {tex}\frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ Z = 1] Thus, the relation between the orbit radius and the electron velocity is {tex}r = \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} The kinetic energy (K) and electrostatic potential energy (U) of the electron in hydrogen atom are {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 8 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} and {tex}U = - \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} (The negative sign in U signifies that the electrostatic force is attractive in nature.) Thus, the total mechanical energy E of the electron in a hydrogen atom is {tex}E = K + U = \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 8 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r } - \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r } = - \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 8 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} The total energy of the electron is negative. This implies the fact that the electron is bound to the nucleus. If E were positive, an electron will not follow a dosed orbit around the nucleus and it would leave the atom.
solution_html:
The Rutherford nuclear model of the atom describes the atom as an electrically neutral sphere consisting of a very small, massive and positively charged nucleus at the centre surrounded by the revolving electrons in their respective dynamically stable orbits. The electrostatic force of attraction F, between the revolving electrons and the nucleus provides the requisite centripetal force (Fc) to keep them in their orbits. Thus, for a dynamically stable orbit in a hydrogen atom
Fc = Fe
{tex}\frac { m v ^ { 2 } } { r } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ Z = 1]
Thus, the relation between the orbit radius and the electron velocity is
{tex}r = \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
The kinetic energy (K) and electrostatic potential energy (U) of the electron in hydrogen atom are
{tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 8 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex}
and {tex}U = - \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex}
(The negative sign in U signifies that the electrostatic force is attractive in nature.)
Thus, the total mechanical energy E of the electron in a hydrogen atom is 
{tex}E = K + U = \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 8 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r } - \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r } = - \frac { e ^ { 2 } } { 8 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex}
The total energy of the electron is negative. This implies the fact that the electron is bound to the nucleus. If E were positive, an electron will not follow a dosed orbit around the nucleus and it would leave the atom.

question_html:
Hydrogen atom in its ground state is excited by means of monochromatic radiation of wavelength {tex}975\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}.

  1. How many different lines are possible in the resulting spectrum?
  2. Calculate the longest wavelength amongst them. You may assume the ionization energy for hydrogen atom as 13.6 eV.
question_text: Hydrogen atom in its ground state is excited by means of monochromatic radiation of wavelength {tex}975\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} . How many different lines are possible in the resulting spectrum? Calculate the longest wavelength amongst them. You may assume the ionization energy for hydrogen atom as 13.6 eV.
explanation: Energy of the ground state (n = 1) = – (ionization energy) = –13.6 eV The wavelength of the incident radiation, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 975 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The energy of the incident photon = hc/ {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{975 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 12.75 eV Let electron is exerted to nth orbit, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12.75 = 13.6 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 4 The quantum transitions to the less excited states gives six possible lines as follows: n = 4 : (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3), (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2), (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1) n = 3 : (3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2), (3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1) n = 2 : (2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1) The longest wavelength emitted is for the transitions (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3) where energy difference is minimum. E min = (E 4 - E 3 ) = 13.6 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right){/tex} = 0.661 eV Thus {tex}\lambda_{\max }=\frac{h c}{\mathrm{E}_{\min }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{0.661 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 18807 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}
solution: Energy of the ground state (n = 1) = – (ionization energy) = –13.6 eV The wavelength of the incident radiation, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 975 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The energy of the incident photon = hc/ {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{975 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 12.75 eV Let electron is exerted to nth orbit, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12.75 = 13.6 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 4 The quantum transitions to the less excited states gives six possible lines as follows: n = 4 : (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3), (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2), (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1) n = 3 : (3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2), (3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1) n = 2 : (2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1) The longest wavelength emitted is for the transitions (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3) where energy difference is minimum. E min = (E 4 - E 3 ) = 13.6 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right){/tex} = 0.661 eV Thus {tex}\lambda_{\max }=\frac{h c}{\mathrm{E}_{\min }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{0.661 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 18807 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Energy of the ground state (n = 1) = – (ionization energy) = –13.6 eV
    The wavelength of the incident radiation, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 975 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The energy of the incident photon = hc/{tex}\lambda{/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{975 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 12.75 eV
    Let electron is exerted to nth orbit,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12.75 = 13.6 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 4
    The quantum transitions to the less excited states gives six possible lines as follows:
    n = 4 : (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3), (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2), (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1)
    n = 3 : (3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2), (3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1)
    n = 2 : (2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1)
  2. The longest wavelength emitted is for the transitions (4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3) where energy difference is minimum.
    Emin = (E4 - E3) = 13.6 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right){/tex} = 0.661 eV
    Thus {tex}\lambda_{\max }=\frac{h c}{\mathrm{E}_{\min }}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{0.661 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
    {tex}\approx{/tex} 18807 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}

question_html: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the orbital period of the electron moving in the nth orbit of the hydrogen atom.
question_text: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the orbital period of the electron moving in the nth orbit of the hydrogen atom.
explanation: Centripetal force = Electrostatic attraction {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{k e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} m v 2 r = k e 2 ...(i) Bohr's quantization condition for angular momentum is {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing (i) by (ii), we get {tex}v=\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \pi m v r}{m v^{2}}=\frac{2 \pi\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\right)}{m\left(\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n^{3} h^{3}}{4 \pi^{2} m k^{2} e^{4}} \quad\left[k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right]{/tex}
solution: Centripetal force = Electrostatic attraction {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{k e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} m v 2 r = k e 2 ...(i) Bohr's quantization condition for angular momentum is {tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing (i) by (ii), we get {tex}v=\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}{/tex} {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \pi m v r}{m v^{2}}=\frac{2 \pi\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\right)}{m\left(\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n^{3} h^{3}}{4 \pi^{2} m k^{2} e^{4}} \quad\left[k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Centripetal force = Electrostatic attraction {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{k e^{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
m v2 r = k e2 ...(i)

Bohr's quantization condition for angular momentum is
{tex}m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} ...(ii)
Dividing (i) by (ii), we get
{tex}v=\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}{/tex}
{tex}T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \pi m v r}{m v^{2}}=\frac{2 \pi\left(\frac{n h}{2 \pi}\right)}{m\left(\frac{2 \pi k e^{2}}{n h}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{n^{3} h^{3}}{4 \pi^{2} m k^{2} e^{4}} \quad\left[k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\right]{/tex}

question_html:
Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the frequency of radiation emitted when electron in hydrogen atom undergoes transition from higher energy state (quantum number ni) to the lower state,(nf).
When electron in hydrogen atom jumps from energy state ni = 4 to nf = 3, 2, 1, identify the spectral series to which the emission lines belong.
question_text: Using Bohr's postulates, derive the expression for the frequency of radiation emitted when electron in hydrogen atom undergoes transition from higher energy state (quantum number n i ) to the lower state,(n f ). When electron in hydrogen atom jumps from energy state n i = 4 to n f = 3, 2, 1, identify the spectral series to which the emission lines belong.
solution_html:
Suppose m be the mass of an electron and v be its speed in nth orbit of radius r. The centripetal force for revolution is produced by electrostatic attraction between electron and nucleus.
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(Z e)(e)}{r^{2}}{/tex} 
or, {tex}m v^{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{r}{/tex} .....(i)
So, Kinetic energy [K] = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex}
K = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r}{/tex}
Potential energy = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(Z e)(-e)}{r}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{r}{/tex}
Total energy, 
E = KE + PE = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r}+\left(-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{r}\right){/tex}
E = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r}{/tex}
So, En = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r_{n}}{/tex} ......(ii)
Again from Bohr's postulate for quantization of angular momentum,
velocity, v = {tex}\frac{nh}{2\pi mr}{/tex}
Substituting this value of v in equation (i), we get
m({tex}\frac{nh}{2\pi mr}{/tex})2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e^{2}}{r}{/tex}
or, r = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{0} h^{2} n^{2}}{\pi m Z e^{2}}{/tex} or rn = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{0} h^{2} n^{2}}{\pi m Z e^{2}}{/tex} ....(iii)
Substituting this value of rn in equation (ii), we get
En = {tex}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{Z e^{2}}{2\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{0} h^{2} n^{2}}{\pi m Z e^{2}}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{m Z^{2} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^2 h^{2} n^{2}}{/tex}
or, En = {tex}-\frac{Z^{2} R h c}{n^{2}}{/tex}, where R = {tex}\frac{m e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} c h^{3}}{/tex}
where R is called Rydberg constant.
For hydrogen atom, Z = 1, so
En = {tex}\frac{-R c h}{n^{2}}{/tex}
If ni and nf are the quantum numbers of initial and final states and Ei & Ef are energies of electron in H-atom in an initial and final state, we have
Ei = {tex}\frac{-R h c}{n_{i}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}E_{f}=\frac{-R h c}{n_{f}^{2}}{/tex}
If {tex}\nu{/tex} is the frequency of emitted radiation, we get
{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{E_{i}-E_{f}}{h}{/tex}
{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-R ch}{n_{i}^{2}}{/tex} - {tex}\left(\frac{-R ch}{n_{f}^{2}}\right){/tex} = Rch{tex}\left[\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{m e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex}{tex}\left[\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
This is the required expression.
If electron jumps from ni = 4 to nf = 3, 2, 1, radiation belongs to Paschen, Balmer and Lyman series.

(i) and (ii), we get {tex}r = n ^ { 2 } \frac { h ^ { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ...........(iii) This is the equation for the radii of the permitted orbits. According to this equation, {tex}r _ { n } \propto n ^ { 2 }{/tex} Force acting on electron due to nucleus is, {tex}F = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 }{/tex} The acceleration of electron = v 2 /r (towards the centre) From Newton's second law, {tex}F = m \left( v ^ { 2 } / r \right) \Rightarrow \frac { z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } } = m \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { r } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 }{/tex} ................(i) From Bohr's quantisation rules, {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} ..........(ii) Substituting the value of r from Eq (i), we get {tex}m v \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( m v ^ { 2 } \right) } = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi } \Rightarrow v = Z e ^ { 2 } / 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n{/tex} ..........(iii) So, kinetic energy, {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ............(iv) Potential energy of the atom, U {tex}= - \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...........(v) Using value of r from (iii) in Eq. (v), we get {tex}U = \frac { - Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( \varepsilon _ { 0 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } / \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { - Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} The total energy in n th energy level of hydrogen atom is given by: {tex}E = K + U = + \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } - \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} i.e total energy of electron varies as {tex}\frac { 1 } { n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .

question_text: Using Bohr's postulates for hydrogen atom, show that the total energy (E) of the electron in the stationary states can be expressed as the sum of kinetic energy (K) and potential energy (U), where K = - 2 U. Hence, deduce the expression for the total energy in the nth energy level of hydrogen atom.
explanation: According to Bohr's postulates for hydrogen atom electron revolves in a circular orbit around the heavy positively charged nucleus. For a particular orbit, electron moves there, so it has kinetic energy. Also, there is potential energy due to charge on electron and heavy positively charged nucleus. Hence, total energy (E) of atom is sum of kinetic energy (K) and potential energy (U). i.e. E = K + U Let us assume that the nucleus has positive charge Ze. An electron moving with a constant speed v along a circle of radius r with centre at the nucleus. Force acting on electron due to nucleus is given by, {tex}F = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 }{/tex} The acceleration of electron = v 2 /r If m = mass of an electron, then from Newton's second law {tex}F = m \left( v ^ { 2 } / r \right) \Rightarrow \frac { z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } } = m \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { r } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 }{/tex} ................(i) From Bohr's quantisation rules {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} ..........(ii) where n is positive integer Substituting the value of r from Eq (i), we get {tex}m v \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( m v ^ { 2 } \right) } = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi } \Rightarrow v = Z e ^ { 2 } / 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n{/tex} ..........(iii) Kinetic energy, {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ............(iv) Potential energy of the atom, U {tex}= - \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...........(v) Using Eq. (iii) in Eq. (i), we get {tex}r = \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m \frac { \left( Z e ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \left( 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n \right) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } { \left( 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \right) m Z e ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow r = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } { \pi m z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Using value of r in Eq. (v), we get {tex}U = \frac { - Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( \varepsilon _ { 0 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } / \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { - Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} This implies, K = -2U So, the total energy in n th energy level of hydrogen atom is {tex}E = K + U = + \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } - \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

For a particular orbit, electron moves there, so it has kinetic energy. Also, there is potential energy due to charge on electron and heavy positively charged nucleus. Hence, total energy (E) of atom is sum of kinetic energy (K) and potential energy (U). i.e. E = K + U Let us assume that the nucleus has positive charge Ze. An electron moving with a constant speed v along a circle of radius r with centre at the nucleus. Force acting on electron due to nucleus is given by, {tex}F = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 }{/tex} The acceleration of electron = v 2 /r If m = mass of an electron, then from Newton's second law {tex}F = m \left( v ^ { 2 } / r \right) \Rightarrow \frac { z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } } = m \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { r } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 }{/tex} ................(i) From Bohr's quantisation rules {tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex} ..........(ii) where n is positive integer Substituting the value of r from Eq (i), we get {tex}m v \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( m v ^ { 2 } \right) } = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi } \Rightarrow v = Z e ^ { 2 } / 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n{/tex} ..........(iii) Kinetic energy, {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ............(iv) Potential energy of the atom, U {tex}= - \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...........(v) Using Eq. (iii) in Eq. (i), we get {tex}r = \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m \frac { \left( Z e ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \left( 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n \right) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } { \left( 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \right) m Z e ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow r = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } { \pi m z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Using value of r in Eq. According to Bohr's postulates for hydrogen atom electron revolves in a circular orbit around the heavy positively charged nucleus.
For a particular orbit, electron moves there, so it has kinetic energy.
Also, there is potential energy due to charge on electron and heavy positively charged nucleus. Hence, total energy (E) of atom is sum of kinetic energy (K) and potential energy (U). i.e. E = K + U
Let us assume that the nucleus has positive charge Ze. An electron moving with a constant speed v along a circle of radius r with centre at the nucleus. Force acting on electron due to nucleus is given by,
{tex}F = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
The acceleration of electron = v2/r 
If m = mass of an electron, then from Newton's second law
{tex}F = m \left( v ^ { 2 } / r \right) \Rightarrow \frac { z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r ^ { 2 } } = m \left( \frac { v ^ { 2 } } { r } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r = Z e ^ { 2 } / 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m v ^ { 2 }{/tex}................(i)
From Bohr's quantisation rules
{tex}m v r = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi }{/tex}..........(ii)
where n is positive integer
Substituting the value of r from Eq (i), we get
{tex}m v \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( m v ^ { 2 } \right) } = n \frac { h } { 2 \pi } \Rightarrow v = Z e ^ { 2 } / 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n{/tex}..........(iii)
Kinetic energy, {tex}K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ............(iv)
Potential energy of the atom, U {tex}= - \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } r }{/tex} ...........(v)
Using Eq. (iii) in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}r = \frac { Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } m \frac { \left( Z e ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \left( 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } h n \right) ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } { \left( 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \right) m Z e ^ { 2 } } \Rightarrow r = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } { \pi m z e ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Using value of r in Eq. (v), we get
{tex}U = \frac { - Z e ^ { 2 } } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } \left( \varepsilon _ { 0 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } / \pi m Z e ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { - Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
This implies, K = -2U
So, the total energy in nth energy level of hydrogen atom is
{tex}E = K + U = + \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } - \frac { m Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } } { 4 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { Z ^ { 2 } e ^ { 4 } m } { 8 \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } h ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Obtain an expression for the frequency of radiations emitted when a hydrogen atom de-excites from level n to level (n - 1). For large n, show that the frequency equals the classical frequency of revolution of the electron in the orbit.
question_text: Obtain an expression for the frequency of radiations emitted when a hydrogen atom de-excites from level n to level (n - 1). For large n, show that the frequency equals the classical frequency of revolution of the electron in the orbit.
solution_html:
The frequency {tex}\nu {/tex} of the emitted radiation when a hydrogen atom de-excites from level n to level (n - 1) is
E = h{tex}\nu {/tex} = E2 - E1
{tex}\nu = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{m{c^2}{\alpha ^2}}}{h}\left[ {\frac{1}{{n_1^2}} - \frac{1}{{n_2^2}}} \right]{/tex} where {tex}\alpha = \frac{{2\pi K{e^2}}}{{ch}}{/tex}
{tex}\nu = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{m{c^2}{\alpha ^2}}}{h}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{{(n - 1)}^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{n^2}}}} \right] = \frac{{m{c^2}{\alpha ^2}}}{{2h}}\left[ {\frac{{{n^2} - {{(n - 1)}^2}}}{{{n^2}{{(n - 1)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{m{c^2}{\alpha ^2}\left[ {(n + n - 1)(n - n + 1)} \right]}}{{2h\;{n^2}{{(n - 1)}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\nu = \frac{{m{c^2}{\alpha ^2}(2n - 1)}}{{2h\;{n^2}{{(n - 1)}^2}}}{/tex}
For large n, (2n - 1) = 2n, and (n - 1) = n
{tex}\nu = \frac{{m{c^2}{\alpha ^2} \cdot 2n}}{{2h\;{n^2} \cdot {n^2}}} = \frac{{m{c^2}{\alpha ^2}}}{{h\;{n^3}}}{/tex}
Putting {tex}\alpha = \frac{{2\pi K{e^2}}}{{ch}}{/tex} we get, {tex}\nu = \frac{{m{c^2}}}{{h\;{n^3}}} \cdot \frac{{4{\pi ^2}{K^2}{e^4}}}{{{c^2}{h^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\nu = \frac{{4{\pi ^2}m{K^2}{e^4}}}{{{n^3}{h^3}}}{/tex} .....(i)
In Bohr's atom model, velocity of electron in nth orbit is {tex}v = \frac{{n\;h}}{{2\pi mr}}{/tex} and radius of nth orbit is {tex}r = \frac{{{n^2}{h^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}mK{e^2}}}(\because Z = 1){/tex}
Frequency of revolution of electron, {tex}\nu = \frac{\upsilon }{{2\pi r}} = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi mr}}\left( {\frac{{4{\pi ^2}mK{e^2}}}{{2\pi .{n^2}{h^2}}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\nu = \frac{{K{e^2}}}{{nh \cdot r}} = \frac{{K{e^2}}}{{nh}}\left( {\frac{{4{\pi ^2}mK{e^2}}}{{{n^2}{h^2}}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\nu = \frac{{4{\pi ^2}m{K^2}{e^4}}}{{{n^3}{h^3}}}{/tex}
which is the same as (i)
Hence for large values of n, classical frequency of revolution of electron in nth orbit is the same as the frequency of radiation emitted when hydrogen atom de-excites from level (n) to level (n - 1).
